Forms of Government Most people generally agree that anarchy, or the absence of organized government, does not facilitate a desirable living environment for society, but it is much harder for individuals to agree upon the particulars of how a population should be governed. Throughout history, various forms of government have evolved to suit the needs of changing populations and mindsets, each with pros and cons. Here, we will distinguish between autocracies and democracies. Autocracies are states that are governed by a single person (or small group of people) who are not constitutionally accountable to the people that they rule. Democracies, by contrast, involve the election of leaders and/or representatives whose job it is to act on the behalf of the voters, and must abide by the constitution or constitutional principles. Although the presence or absence of competitive elections is the key difference between these two types of government, in practice there is much variation within each regime type. For example, Cuba and China are autocratic states where elections are held, but the voters are only able to vote for the Communist party. Likewise there are many democracies around the world where the leaders are directly elected, but do not always adhere to constitutional principles. Autocracy: Monarchy, Civilian Dictatorship and Oligarchy Even though people in the United States tend to be most aware of Great Britain’s royals, many other nations also recognize kings, queens, princes, princesses, and other figures with official royal titles. The power held by these positions varies from one country to another. Strictly speaking, a monarchy is a government in which a single person (a monarch) rules until he or she dies or abdicates the throne. Usually, a monarch claims the rights to the title by way of hereditary succession or as a result of some sort of divine appointment or calling. The monarchies of most modern nations are ceremonial remnants of tradition, and individuals who hold titles in such sovereignties are often aristocratic figureheads. This means they are not generally involved in governmental decision-making, but instead hold symbolic or ceremonial positions of authority. A few nations today, however, are run by governments wherein a monarch has absolute or unmitigated power. Such nations are called absolute monarchies. The small, oil-rich nation of Oman, for instance, is an example of an absolute monarchy. In this nation, Sultan Qaboos bin Said ruled from the 1970s until his death in 2020, when his cousin, Haitham bin Tariq, became Sultan. The Sultan creates all laws, appoints all judges, and has no formal check on their power. Figure 10.16 Qaboos bin Said ruled Oman as its absolute monarch for fifty years, and oversaw the country's development from a relatively isolated nation to one that uses its vast supplies of oil to build wealth and influence. Queen Noor of Jordan is the dowager queen of this constitutional monarchy and has limited political authority. Queen Noor is American by birth but relinquished her citizenship when she married. She is a noted global advocate for Arab-Western relations. (Credit A: Wikimedia Commons; B: Skoll World Forum/flickr)

In today’s global landscape, monarchies far more often take the form of constitutional monarchies, governments of nations that recognize monarchs but require these figures to abide by the laws of a national constitution. Many countries that are now constitutional monarchies evolved from governments that were once absolute monarchies. In most cases, constitutional monarchies, such as Great Britain and Canada, feature elected prime ministers whose leadership role in governance is far more involved and significant than that of its titled monarchs. In spite of their limited authority, monarchs endure in part because people enjoy their ceremonial significance and the pageantry of their rites and regard the monarch as a symbol of national unity. Power in a dictatorship is held by a single person or a very small group that wields complete and absolute authority over a government and population. Like some absolute monarchies, dictatorships may be corrupt and seek to limit or even eradicate the liberties of the general population. Dictators use a variety of means to perpetuate their authority. Some “benevolent” dictators, such as Napoleon and Anwar Sadat, are credited with advancing their people’s standard of living or exercising a moderate amount of evenhandedness. Others lead through fear and intimidation and gross abuses of their power, such as Joseph Stalin, Adolf Hitler, Saddam Hussein, Cambodia’s Pol Pot, and Zimbabwe’s Robert Mugabe, for instance. Some dictators also possess the personal appeal that Max Weber identified with a charismatic, or personalist, leader. Subjects of a personalist civilian dictator may believe that the leader has special abilities or powers, and on that basis are willing to submit to his or her authority. This form of political power is rooted in the belief that the leader themselves hold unique capacity to lead and rule the country, rather than power derived from institutions, the law, or inherited authority. The late Kim Jong-Il, North Korean dictator, and his successor, Kim Jong-Un, exemplify this type of charismatic dictatorship. The North Korean propaganda portrayed these men as military geniuses, morally and spiritually pure, and uniquely capable of protecting the nation’s sovereignty. In extreme cases, leaders may attempt to establish totalitarian control, when the state attempts to exert control over all aspects of public and private life including occupation, religious beliefs, and number of children permitted in each family. Oligarchy The power in an oligarchy is held by a small, elite group. Unlike in a monarchy, members of an oligarchy do not necessarily achieve their statuses based on ties to noble ancestry. Rather, they may ascend to positions of power because of military might, economic power, or similar circumstances. The concept of oligarchy is somewhat elusive; rarely does a society openly define itself as an oligarchy. Generally, the word carries negative connotations and conjures notions a small group of “elites” who hold extensive power in the

Figure 10.17 Dictator Kim Jong-Il of North Korea was a charismatic leader of an absolute dictatorship. His followers responded emotionally to the death of their leader in 2011. (Credit: babeltrave/flickr)

government and in policy decisions. In practice, oligarchies can exist in either autocratic or democratic states. Oligarchies have existed throughout history, and today many consider Russia an example of oligarchic political structure. After the fall of communism, groups of business owners captured control of this nation’s natural resources and have used the opportunity to expand their wealth and political influence. Once an oligarchic power structure is established, it can be very challenging for middle- and lower-class citizens to advance their interests in representative democracy. These concerns were raised by C Wright Wells in his 1956 book “Power Elite” as described in Chapter 8. More recently, University of California Santa Cruz Professor of Sociology D. William Domhoff has argued that the United States faces more challenges than in the past with the concentration of political power in the hands of a small group of wealthy individuals (see https://whorulesamerica.ucsc.edu/). A key reason is related to campaign finance laws. The Supreme Court decision for Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission in 2010 created pathways for “corporations” (as defined by each state) to legally (and more easily) contribute to political campaigns. As a result, corporations play an outsized role in the U.S. political system. In the most recent major U.S. elections, a small group of very wealthy individuals, largely associated with the technology industry, have significantly influenced government decision-making through these political donations. President Biden expressed deep concerns about the United States behaving like an oligarchy government (Bekiempis, 2025). Despite these concerns, during the 2024 presidential election, both Donald Trump and Kamala Harris received significant financial support from wealthy donors. According to OpenSecrets.org, almost 60% of the funding Kamala Harris raised for her campaign came from large donations. However, 69% of Trump’s campaign funding came from large donations (including many large technology companies) and the largest individual contribution came from SpaceX Corporation ($276M) which is owned by billionaire Elon Musk. When Trump ultimately was elected and took office in 2025, several technology executives involved in the fundraising for his campaign were subsequently invited to participate in important policy roles. The most well-known example was Elon Musk, who was involved in identifying sweeping government spending cuts in the early months of President Trump’s second term, and has experienced well documented financial benefits from Trump’s policy decisions. Democracy A democracy is a form of government that strives to provide all citizens with an equal voice, or vote, in determining state policy. At minimum, a democracy selects its government leaders through elections where the winner is not known ahead of time, and the loser of the election allows for the peaceful transfer of power (Prezworski 1991). Voters can participate in elections by casting their ballot for their preferred representative or party. These representatives may occupy positions in the executive branch, like a president or a prime minister, or in the legislature. Often, the rules that govern the elections, the responsibility of representatives in different offices, and the rights of citizens are written in a
Figure 10.18 The Breakers, the famous Newport, Rhode Island, home of the Vanderbilts, is a powerful symbol of the extravagant wealth that characterized the Gilded Age. (Credit: ckramer/flickr)

national constitution.1 Whereas in autocracies, the government leaders rule by the authority given by birth (monarchy) or coercive power (dictatorships), leaders in democracies are authorized to govern because the public voted them into office. Democracies, in general, aim to establish and protect certain basic rights for their citizens. First and foremost, citizens are free to organize political parties and participate in elections. Leaders, once elected, must abide by the terms of the nation’s constitution and are limited in the powers they can exercise, as well as in the length of the duration of their terms. Most democratic societies also champion freedom of individual speech, the press, and assembly, and they prohibit unlawful imprisonment. Of course, even in a democratic society, the government constrains citizens' total freedom to act however they wish. A democratically elected government does this by passing laws and writing regulations that establish limits on lawful behavior, and, at least ideally, reflect the will of the majority of its people. Democracy in the United States Lincoln's famous phrase “of the people, by the people, for the people” is at the heart of the U.S. system and sums up its most essential aspect: that citizens willingly and freely elect executive and legislative representatives they believe will look out for their best interests. Elections in the United States are competitive, and contested by candidates where multiple parties have a chance at winning, the results of which are generally accepted without controversy. Although many Americans take free elections for granted, it is a vital foundation of any democracy. In most elections, voter participation in the U.S. is quite low, and sociologists explore the complex social issues that influence political participation. This can have consequences for representation, and informs the larger policy making process. Voter Participation and Representation Insofar that elections determine which candidates and parties are elected to government, voter participation is essential to the policy-making process and representation in any democracy, and the U.S. is no exception. Although many Americans are quick to complain about laws and political leadership, in any given election year roughly half the population does not vote (Ballotopedia, 2025). Some election years have seen even lower turnouts; in 2010, for instance, only 37.8 percent of the adult population participated in the electoral process (United States Elections Project 2011). More recently, voter turnout in national elections has increased, with more than 60% of eligible voters casting votes in 2020 and 2024 (Pew Research Center 2025). Race, Gender, and Class & Voter Participation Although recent records have shown more minorities voting now than ever before, this trend is still fairly new. Some determinants of voter participation are rooted in historical precedent. When the U.S. government was formed, African Americans and women were denied the right to vote in most states. Black men were usually not allowed to vote at all until after the Civil War, and Black women gained the right to vote along with other women only with the ratification of the 19th Amendment in 1920. The 15th Amendment (1870) established that all men were permitted to vote, irrespective of race or color, while the 19th Amendment (1920) extended the right of suffrage to all women. Further legal

1 Constitutions that delineate the roles and responsibilities of leaders and citizens alike can be found in many democratic and some autocratic states. Some democracies, such as the United Kingdom and Israel, do not have a written constitution.

Figure 10.19 Americans' voting rights are a fundamental element of the U.S. democratic structure. In elections people care about, the turnout can be very high, and people go to great lengths to ensure their vote is counted. (Credit: GPA Photo Archive/flickr)

protections were codified to protect minority voters in both the 1964 Civil Rights Act and the 1965 Voting Rights Act, that would establish equal suffrage in federal statute. 2 Yet even following these constitutional amendments, African American participation was discouraged by discriminatory legislation, passed in many southern states, which required poll taxes and literacy tests of prospective voters. Literacy tests were not outlawed until 1965, when President Lyndon Johnson signed the Voting Rights Act. Today, there are some enduring patterns to voter turnout across different groups of people. Voter participation of White non-Hispanics as a proportion of the eligible voters (citizens) exceeds that of Blacks, Asians, and Hispanics, and it has done so since the 1970’s (U.S. Census Bureau, 2024). The one noted exception to this trend is in 2012, when the Barack Obama became the first African American to be elected as president. In that year, African Americans turned out to vote at levels higher than other racial groups: although 61.8% of eligible voters turned out to vote in that election, 66.2% of African Americans reported their participation. Curiously, we find a different pattern as it relates to men and women’s voting participation. Despite their historic exclusion from suffrage prior to the 19th Amendment, women tend to vote at higher rates than American men. In every election since 1982, a higher percentage of eligible women have shown up to the polls, as opposed to men (U.S. Census Bureau, 2024). Like race and ethnicity, social class also has influenced voter participation. Voter turnout among lower-educated, lower- paid workers is lower than for people with higher socioeconomic status. Several explanations have been offered to account for this difference (Raymond 2010). For example, workers in low-paying service jobs might find it harder to get to the polls because they lack flexibility in their work hours, or quality daycare to look after children while they vote. In addition, the overrepresentation of Black men in the criminal justice system already reduces their voting capacity, leading to greater disenfranchisement among black men (Uggen et al, 2023). Race, Gender, and Class & Representation The extent to which differences in voter participation translates to differences in representation can be difficult to determine, in part for various ways we might define representation. Partisan representation is the extent to which votes cast for a given party is translated into legislative seats or executive positions. In U.S. elections, for example, most elections are contested between candidates from one of the two major political parties, which then control roughly equal shares of seats in the United States Congress. Substantive representation refers to the extent to which the laws and regulations that are passed reflect what citizens or voters want. Political scientists study electoral laws and systems to understand how different voting rules and laws affect the translation of votes to seats (Taagepera and Shugart 1989; Cox 1997), and what this might imply for representation (Powell 2000). A sociological perspective might emphasize descriptive representation, which is the extent to which the characteristics of representatives ‘reflect’ that of the electorate in terms of race, gender, class and other social groupings. Evidence suggests that the right to vote and voter participation does not directly translate into descriptive representation. Relative to their presence in the U.S. and voting population, women and racial/ethnic minorities are underrepresented throughout all levels of government. White men still dominate both chambers in the U.S. Congress, and until the inauguration of President Obama in 2009, all U.S. presidents had been White men. How descriptive representation relates to party or substantive representation is the subject of much debate. On the one hand, although African Americans have historically favored the Democratic party, and since 2012 Latinos/Hispanics have tended to vote for Republican candidates (Pew Research Center 2024). And, substantial proportions of women

2 In June 2013 the Supreme Court repealed several aspects of the 1965 Voting Rights Act, ruling that southern states no longer need the federal oversight that was once required to prohibit racial discrimination in voting practices in the South. Following this decision, several states moved forward with voter identification laws that had previously been banned by federal courts. Officials in Texas, Mississippi, and Alabama claim that new identification (ID) laws are needed to reduce voter fraud. Opponents argue that voter fraud is rare and that voter ID laws disproportionately affect minorities and the poor.

So while minority voters might prioritize their racial identity in some contexts, it is not the only consideration they have when going to the ballot box (c.f. Hopkins, Kaiser, Perez 2023; Perez, Lee and Luna 2025). At the same time, research documents ways in which ‘who’ is elected to office may be consequential for policymaking. Working class representatives’ preferences tend to align with the perspectives of groups they are from, owing to shared lived experiences (Carnes and Lupu 2015; Barnes, Keverell and Saxton 2023). Butler and Broockman (2011) show that although U.S. state legislators from both major parties were on average less responsive to constituents they believe to be African American, yet minority representatives are more likely to respond to minority constituent requests, even from outside their district (Butler and Broockman 2011). Finally, research suggests that women’s equal presence is consequential for perceived legitimacy of government institutions and processes: both men and women more trusting of an institution, and more accepting of its policy output, when both women and men are equally involved in the policy- making process (Clayton, O’Brien and Piscopo (2018). Although voting is the foundation to representation in the U.S. government, there are other means to make one’s voice heard. The First Amendment to the U.S. Constitution states that “Congress shall make no law…abridging the freedom of speech.” This right to free speech can be influential, meaning that people can participate in the democratic process by volunteering with political advocacy groups, writing letter to their elected officials, or sharing their views in a public forum such as a blog or letter to the editor. Americans as also free to form or join political organizations, advocacy and interest groups, to participate in public demonstrations, and even run for an elected office. Theoretical Perspectives on Government and Power Functionalism, conflict perspective, and symbolic interactionism offer distinct lenses through which sociologists study government and political power in society. Functionalism According to functionalism, the government has four main purposes: planning and directing society, meeting social needs, maintaining law and order, and managing international relations. Functionalists view government and politics as a way to enforce norms and regulate conflict. Functionalists see active social change, such as the sit-in on Wall Street, as undesirable because it forces change and, as a result, undesirable things that might have to be compensated for. Functionalists seek consensus and order in society. Dysfunction creates social problems that lead to social change. For instance, functionalists would see monetary political contributions as a way of keeping people connected to the democratic process. This would be in opposition to a conflict theorist who would see this financial contribution as a way for the rich to perpetuate their own wealth. Conflict Perspective The conflict perspective focuses on the social inequalities and power differences within a group. Philosopher and social scientist Karl Marx believed that conflict between groups struggling to either attain wealth and power or keep the wealth and power they had was inevitable in a capitalist society, and conflict was the only way for the underprivileged to eventually gain some measure of equality. C. Wright Mills (1956) elaborated on some of Marx’s concepts, coining the phrase “the power elite” to describe what he saw as the small group of powerful people who control much of a society. Mills believed the power elite use government to develop social policies

Figure 10.20 Although military technology has evolved considerably over the course of history, the fundamental causes of conflict among nations remain essentially the same. (Credit: Wikimedia Commons)

that allow them to keep their wealth. Contemporary theorist G. William Domhoff (2011) elaborates on ways in which the power elite may be seen as a subculture whose members follow similar social patterns such as joining elite clubs, attending select schools, and vacationing at a handful of exclusive destinations. Conflict perspective in Action Even before there were modern nation-states, political conflicts arose among competing societies or factions of people. Nearly all conflicts in the past and present are spurred by basic desires: the drive to protect or gain territory and wealth, and the need to preserve liberty and autonomy. According to sociologist and philosopher Karl Marx, such conflicts are necessary, although ugly, steps toward a more egalitarian society. Marx saw a historical pattern in which revolutionaries toppled elite power structures, after which wealth and authority became more evenly dispersed among the population, and the overall social order advanced. In this pattern of change through conflict, people tend to gain greater personal freedom and economic stability (1848). Modern-day conflicts are still driven by the desire to gain or protect power and wealth, whether in the form of land and resources or in the form of liberty and autonomy. Symbolic Interactionism Other sociologists study government and power by relying on the framework of symbolic interactionism, which is grounded in the works of Max Weber and George H. Mead. Symbolic interactionism, as it pertains to government, focuses its attention on figures, emblems, or individuals that represent power and authority. Many diverse entities in larger society can be considered symbolic: trees, doves, wedding rings. Images that represent the power and authority of the United States include the White House, the eagle, and the American flag. The Seal of the President of the United States, along with the office in general, incites respect and reverence in many Americans. Symbolic interactionists are not interested in large structures such as the government. As micro-sociologists, they are more interested in the face-to-face aspects of politics. In reality, much of politics consists of face-to-face backroom meetings and lobbyist efforts. What the public often sees is the front porch of politics that is sanitized by the media through gatekeeping. Symbolic interactionists are most interested in the interaction between these small groups who make decisions, or in the case of some recent congressional committees, demonstrate the inability to make any decisions at all. The heart of politics is the result of interaction between individuals and small groups over periods of time. These meetings produce new meanings and perspectives that individuals use to make sure there are future interactions. Conclusion Across societies, public institutions play central roles in organizing collective life. They guide moral frameworks, transmit knowledge, regulate behavior, and provide the context in which individuals learn to navigate social expectations. The key point of this chapter is that these organizations are not simply background structures; they actively shape the lives of individuals and the character of communities. Their influence can be stabilizing, restrictive, empowering, or contested, depending on historical circumstances and social position. Sociologists analyze these institutions through several complementary perspectives. A functionalist approach emphasizes their integrative roles: religions create shared meaning, schools prepare people for participation in social

Figure 10.21 What symbols of the Boston Tea Party are represented in this painting? How might a symbolic interactionist explain the way the modern-day Tea Party has reclaimed and repurposed these symbolic meanings? (Credit: Wikimedia Commons)

and economic life, and governments coordinate collective decision-making. A conflict perspective draws attention to the unequal distribution of resources and authority, showing how institutions may preserve advantages for some while limiting opportunities for others. Symbolic interactionism highlights the small-scale, everyday experiences that give these institutions their lived reality—how people interpret rules, rituals, and roles, and how those interpretations shape identity and behavior. This chapter also highlights the essential role of social science in investigating these institutions. Researchers rely on evidence-based methods to measure impacts that may be difficult to observe directly: how curriculum shapes students’ expectations, how laws influence behavior, or how religious symbols frame social interaction. These scientific tools allow scholars to move beyond assumptions and uncover the patterns that shape human life. The chapter offers only a brief introduction to topics that have occupied generations of sociologists, but it provides a foundation for understanding how deeply public institutions influence social life and why studying them remains vital.

Key Terms absolute monarchies: governments wherein a monarch has absolute or unmitigated power anarchy: the absence of any organized government authority: power that people accept because it comes from a source that is perceived as legitimate charismatic authority: power legitimized on the basis of a leader’s exceptional personal qualities constitutional monarchies: national governments that recognize monarchs but require these figures to abide by the laws of a greater constitution cultural capital: cultural knowledge that serves (metaphorically) as currency to help one navigate a culture cultural transmission: the way people come to learn the values, beliefs, and social norms of their culture cults: religious groups that are small, secretive, and highly controlling of members and have a charismatic leader denomination: a large, mainstream religion that is not sponsored by the state democracy: a form of government that provides all citizens with an equal voice or vote in determining state policy dictatorship: a form of government in which a single person (or a very small group) wields complete and absolute authority over a government or populace after the dictator rises to power, usually through economic or military might education: a social institution through which a society’s children are taught basic academic knowledge, learning skills, and cultural norms formal education: the learning of academic facts and concepts hidden curriculum: the type of nonacademic knowledge that people learn through informal learning and cultural transmission informal education: education that involves learning about cultural values, norms, and expected behaviors through participation in a society megachurch: a Christian church that has a very large congregation averaging more than 2,000 people who attend regular weekly services monarchy: a form of government in which a single person (a monarch) rules until that individual dies or abdicates the throne

No Child Left Behind Act: an act that requires states to test students in prescribed grades, with the results of those tests determining eligibility to receive federal funding oligarchy: a form of government in which power is held by a small, elite group patrimonialism: a type of authority wherein military and administrative factions enforce the power of the master politics: a means of studying a nation’s or group’s underlying social norms as values as evidenced through its political structure and practices power: the ability to exercise one’s will over others power elite: a small group of powerful people who control much of a society rational-legal authority: power that is legitimized by rules, regulations, and laws religion: a system of beliefs, values, and practices concerning what a person holds to be sacred or spiritually significant religious beliefs: specific ideas that members of a particular faith hold to be true religious experience: the conviction or sensation that one is connected to “the divine” religious rituals: behaviors or practices that are either required for or expected of the members of a particular group representative democracy: a government wherein citizens elect officials to represent their interests sorting: classifying students based on academic merit or potential tracking: a formalized sorting system that places students on “tracks” (advanced, low achievers) that perpetuate inequalities traditional authority: power legitimized on the basis of long-standing customs
